body.u: As I started gazing out of my tiny basement flat window, I could see the children coming home from school. The comprehensive school stood at the top of the hill. As the teenagers stroldstrolled down the hill, talking and laughing in rasedraised voices. I began to remember the day’sdays of my teens back in 1978.

At the age of sixteen I thought I had the weight of the world on my shoulders and all the worries in the world. I left school and began the long hard search for a job. WrittingWriting to every known employer and a few unknown, asking, if they had any vacancesvacancies.

I had just turned seventeen when I recievedreceived a letter, asking me to start work in “Midlands Bank” on October 7th 1978. This seemed to be the outlet for the worries I had carried since July 1978. The small brown envelope mentmeant so much to me. I knew then that the pressure laid upon me in the last year of school was worth every minute spend worried and unsettled.

Working life was so different from what I had expected. There was so many new things to learn and understand, far different from those taught at school. You had to learn, how to treat someone whoswho's temper got the better of them, and how to work with someone giving you direct orders. This I found hard, but as time passed I learnt how to cope with each individual problem that arose.

For the first two years the job was pleasant, but repetertiverepetitive. Being only exciting and challenging. But yet again I had to settle for second best. I began working in a large cloths factory. This seemed strange to every one but me. Some how I enjoyed the factory work much better than banking. Because after each days work I felt that I had achieved something seeing a complete garment ready for the racks in the shops.
At the age of twenty-five I was married. I gave up my factory job to start a family of my own.
With my family I experienced all the factors of motherhood. Watching my children grow and become teenagers themselves and sharing their problems.

After my family had families of their own there was only two to look after and we began to have all those little things we had to forefitforfeit for the children. I could no longer work, for I had reached the age of sixty. The age that seemed so far away when I was sixteen, but here it is.
Still gazing out of the window, I wondered if those sixteen years olds ever wonder what itsit's like to be sixty, and think about the children they see passing their windows.
I once again return to the tiny plot of land at the back of the house I call my garden.
